skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification for parallel feature development
skill.summary: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 42 patterns but all are false positives. The skill is documentation-only (markdown file) containing bash command examples for teaching git worktrees. No executable code exists - all flagged patterns are code blocks in instructional content. External commands are git/bash examples, filesystem references are documentation paths, and crypto warnings are spurious.

content.user_title: Create Isolated Git Workspaces for Parallel Development
content.value_statement: Git worktrees enable simultaneous branch work without context switching. This skill provides systematic directory selection and safety verification for reliable workspace isolation.
actual_capabilities: Select worktree directory using priority order: existing directories, CLAUDE.md config, or user preference
Verify project-local directories are git-ignored before worktree creation
Auto-detect project type and run appropriate setup commands (npm, cargo, pip, go)
Run baseline tests to verify clean worktree state before feature work
Create worktrees with new branches linked to parent repository
Report worktree location and test status upon completion
limitations: Requires git repository with worktree support (git 2.5+)
Cannot create worktrees if parent repository is bare or shallow cloned
Project setup auto-detection limited to Node.js, Rust, Python, and Go projects
Worktrees share .git directory - disk space savings but potential lock conflicts

use_cases.title: Feature Development Isolation
use_cases.description: Create isolated workspace for new feature work without disrupting main development branch
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers working on multiple features simultaneously

use_cases.title: Code Review Preparation
use_cases.description: Set up clean worktree for reviewing pull requests without affecting current work state
use_cases.target_user: Senior developers conducting code reviews while maintaining active development

use_cases.title: Bug Fix Parallelization
use_cases.description: Quickly spin up isolated environment for urgent hotfixes while feature work continues
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers and team leads managing production incidents

prompt_templates.title: Basic Worktree Creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new git worktree for feature branch 'feature/user-auth' using the standard directory selection process
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting standard feature work with no existing worktree configuration

prompt_templates.title: Worktree with Custom Location
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a worktree for 'hotfix/login-bug' in ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/myproject/ for global access
prompt_templates.scenario: Need worktree accessible from multiple project checkouts

prompt_templates.title: Worktree Verification and Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create worktree for 'feature/api-v2', verify it is properly ignored, run project setup, and confirm tests pass before I begin implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Production feature requiring full safety verification baseline

prompt_templates.title: Existing Worktree Reuse
prompt_templates.prompt: Check if worktree 'feature/payment-integration' already exists in .worktrees/, and if so switch to it instead of creating a new one
prompt_templates.scenario: Resuming work on previously created worktree

output_examples.output:
Worktree ready at /Users/jesse/myproject/.worktrees/auth
Tests passing (47 tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement auth feature

output_examples.input: Set up isolated workspace for code review
output_examples.output:
Found existing .worktrees/ directory (git-ignored)
Created worktree: git worktree add .worktrees/review-pr123 -b review/pr-123
Dependencies installed (npm install)
Baseline verified: 52 tests passing

best_practices: Always verify worktree directory is git-ignored before creation to prevent accidental commits
Run baseline tests immediately after worktree creation to distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
Follow directory priority: existing directories first, then CLAUDE.md config, then ask user
anti_patterns: Creating worktrees without verifying git-ignore status causes repository pollution
Skipping baseline test verification makes bug attribution impossible
Hardcoding directory paths violates project conventions and team workflows

faq.question: What is a git worktree and why use it?
faq.answer: A git worktree is a linked working directory that shares the same repository .git folder. It allows you to work on multiple branches simultaneously without switching branches in your main directory.

faq.question: How does this skill choose where to create worktrees?
faq.answer: Priority order: 1) Existing .worktrees/ or worktrees/ directory, 2) CLAUDE.md configuration, 3) Ask user. Project-local directories must be git-ignored before use.

faq.question: Do worktrees duplicate my repository on disk?
faq.answer: No. Worktrees share the .git directory with the main repository, saving disk space. Only the checked-out files occupy additional space.

faq.question: What happens if baseline tests fail in a new worktree?
faq.answer: The skill reports failures and asks whether to proceed or investigate. This distinguishes pre-existing test failures from bugs introduced during feature work.

faq.question: Can I use worktrees with any git repository?
faq.answer: Worktrees require git 2.5 or later. They do not work with bare repositories or shallow clones. Most standard repositories support worktrees.

faq.question: How do I clean up a worktree when finished?
faq.answer: Use the finishing-a-development-branch skill which handles worktree cleanup, branch merging if needed, and proper git housekeeping.
